text: AI systems are rapidly being integrated into all aspects of our daily lives. While they hold a lot of promise, they also create a plethora of new opportunities and risks for ourselves and for our societies. Several regulatory bodies across the globe are now attempting to impose numerous sets of regulatory standards in a bid to minimize the negative impact of future advancements in the technology. A large number of countries have generally relied on recommendations and ethical standards in a non-binding manner, and this is still ongoing. However, the European Union recently issued its regulatory code of standards for all types of AI systems through the issuance of the Artificial Intelligence Act. This Act was ratified by the European Union in early 2024 and became effective on 1st of August, 2024, and is expected to fully implement its objectives within the next two years. The new EU AI Act sets itself apart from other regional regulatory standards that have only dealt with the development and sale of specific types of applications and has instead introduced a more formal and horizontal method of imposing penalties and set regulatory standards on the development of all types of AI systems in all sectors, thereby becoming a comprehensive formal regulation that deals with all aspects of the regulation of AI systems. The European Union’s move is truly pioneering as it is currently the first formal set of regulatory standards that have ever existed. And because its scope of application is arguably also global, in light of the fact that AI systems can also be marketed globally without restriction and hence, in practice, falls under the EU’s competence, the European Union is likely to impose the international benchmark for the development and marketing of all types of AI systems across the world.
